coredata.dc:title: High-performance nitrogen-doped green fluorescent carbon dots for applications in rapid detection of chlortetracycline and fluorescent film
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Jing
Long, Xiang
Wu, Shaogui
coredata.dc:description: Chlortetracycline (CTC), a widely used antibiotic, poses potential health risks due to its residues in animal products, making its detection crucial for environmental and human health. This study introduces a novel, rapid, and sensitive method for CTC detection utilizing nitrogen-doped green fluorescent carbon dots (G-CDs) synthesized through a simple hydrothermal process. The G-CDs exhibit strong green fluorescence and high quantum yield, demonstrating excellent selectivity and sensitivity for CTC detection. An investigation of the fluorescence quenching mechanism revealed a combination of static quenching and the inner filter effect (IFE). Additionally, the incorporation of G-CDs into a polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) film resulted in a fluorescent hybrid polymer film with potential applications in optoelectronics. This research not only provides a cost-effective and reliable approach for CTC detection but also expands the utility of high-fluorescence carbon dots in practical applications, contributing to advancements in both environmental monitoring and materials science.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Methyl orange
Carbon dots
Hydrothermal method
Chlortetracycline detection
Fluorescent film

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Flow chart of G-CDs synthesis and (b) possible reaction mechanism diagram.
Optimization of synthesis conditions. Fluorescence spectra of G-CDs at (a) different preparation temperatures and (b) different preparation time.

Structural characterization of G-CDs. (a) TEM images, HRTEM (lower right inset) and particle size distribution (upper right inset); (b) FT-IR spectrum of G-CDs; (c) XPS survey spectrum; (d-f) C1s, N1s, O1s fine spectra, respectively.

Optical properties of G-CDs. (a) Three-dimensional excitation and emission spectra of G-CDs; (b) Two-dimensional projection; (c) Ultraviolet–visible absorption spectrum (red), excitation spectrum (blue), emission spectrum (green), Images of G-CDs under both sunlight and ultraviolet light (upper right insert); (d) Normalized emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths.
The quantum yield of G-CDs. (a) The relationship between the integrated fluorescence intensity and the absorbance in different solvents; (b) The quantum yields of G-CDs in different solvents.
Stability of G-CDs. The effects of (a) salt concentration; (b) UV lamp irradiation; (c) temperature; (d) time on the fluorescence intensity of G-CDs.

The detection of chlortetracycline by G-CDs as chemical sensors. (a) The effects of different metal ions, antibiotics and amino acids on the fluorescence quenching of G-CDs; (b) Changes in fluorescence intensity of G-CDs when CTC coexists with interferents; (c) The effect of chlortetracycline concentration on the fluorescence intensity of G-CDs; (d) The relationship between the concentration of chlortetracycline and (F 0-F)/F 0.

Mechanism analysis of G-CDs detection of CTC. (a) The UV absorption spectra of G-CDs, CTC, and G-CDs+CTC; (b) The fluorescence lifetime of G-CDs and G-CDs+CTC; (c) The UV absorption spectrum of CTC and the maximum excitation spectrum of G-CDs.

Application of G-CDs in fluorescent films (a) Photographs of PVA/G-CDs films under 395 nm UV irradiation; (b) Photographs of PVA/G-CDs films under visible light; photos of (c-e) PVA/G-CDs films under different conditions.
(a) Ultraviolet absorption spectra of PVA/G-CDs and PVA films; (b) Emission spectra of PVA/G-CDs films at different excitation wavelengths.
Comparison with previously reported probes in the literature.
Determination of chlortetracycline content in actual samples.

head.ce:title: High-performance nitrogen-doped green fluorescent carbon dots for applications in rapid detection of chlortetracycline and fluorescent film

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Sichuan Normal University, Chengdu 610066, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Sichuan Normal University, Chengdu 610066, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Chlortetracycline (CTC), a widely used antibiotic, poses potential health risks due to its residues in animal products, making its detection crucial for environmental and human health. This study introduces a novel, rapid, and sensitive method for CTC detection utilizing nitrogen-doped green fluorescent carbon dots (G-CDs) synthesized through a simple hydrothermal process. The G-CDs exhibit strong green fluorescence and high quantum yield, demonstrating excellent selectivity and sensitivity for CTC detection. An investigation of the fluorescence quenching mechanism revealed a combination of static quenching and the inner filter effect (IFE). Additionally, the incorporation of G-CDs into a polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) film resulted in a fluorescent hybrid polymer film with potential applications in optoelectronics. This research not only provides a cost-effective and reliable approach for CTC detection but also expands the utility of high-fluorescence carbon dots in practical applications, contributing to advancements in both environmental monitoring and materials science.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Chlortetracycline (CTC), a widely used antibiotic for treating human infectious diseases, has gained popularity among farmers for its growth-promoting effects in livestock. However, the misuse and overuse of CTC and its analogues in animal feed, particularly those with extended half-lives, have led to significant concerns. This abuse results in excessive drug residues in animal products and contributes to the development of bacterial resistance in animals. Furthermore, residual CTC can pose serious threats to human health through bioaccumulation and other processes, potentially causing adverse effects such as hepatotoxicity and gastrointestinal disorders. Given these risks, the development of effective and reliable analytical methods for detecting CTC is of paramount importance for safeguarding both environmental integrity and human health [1]. This research aims to address this critical need by establishing innovative techniques for CTC detection, thereby contributing to the broader goals of food safety, public health protection, and environmental conservation.

Traditional methods for detecting CTC, such as high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [2,3], immunoassays [4], and capillary electrophoresis [5,6], while effective, are often hindered by their reliance on expensive, bulky instrumentation and complex operational procedures. In contrast, fluorescence-based analytical methods have emerged as a promising alternative, offering simplicity, rapid response, and high sensitivity for CTC detection. The advent of fluorescent nanomaterials has accelerated the development of these methods, with CDs playing a significant role. CDs, a novel class of carbon nanomaterials with diameters below 10 nm, have garnered considerable attention due to their unique optical and electronic properties [7]. Compared with traditional semiconductor quantum dots and organic fluorescent dyes, CDs offer advantages such as easy functionalization [8],low toxicity [9,10], excellent water dispersibility [11], adjustable fluorescence emission [12,13], excellent photostability [14], upconversion [15], and biocompatibility [16,17]. Therefore, they have shown potential applications in the fields of biological imaging [18,19], in vivo therapy [20], drug delivery [21,22], light-emitting diodes [23], photocatalytic solar cells [24], and heavy metal ion detection [25,26]. Numerous studies have reported the application of CDs for the CTC detection. For instance, Chiu et al. developed a selective detection of chlortetracycline using carbon dots synthesized from diethylenetriaminepentaacetic acid and ethylenediamine as precursors [27]. Zhao et al. prepared CDs by hawthorn and established a rapid fluorescence sensor for the detection of chlortetracycline in pork samples [28]. Ren et al. developed an inverse microemulsion method for CTC detection using nitrogen-doped CDs [29]. However, these approaches often suffer from drawbacks such as complex preparation methods, intricate detection procedures, and relatively high detection limits. To address these limitations, our study proposes a novel method employing graphene carbon dots (G-CDs) for CTC detection, which offers advantages in terms of speed, efficiency, accuracy, and cost-effectiveness.

In this study, we synthesized highly fluorescent green-emitting carbon dots (G-CDs) using a facile hydrothermal method, with methyl orange as the carbon source and ethylenediamine as the nitrogen dopant. The resulting water-soluble G-CDs demonstrate strong green fluorescence with a remarkable quantum yield (QY) of 37.1 % and excellent fluorescence stability. These G-CDs demonstrate significant potential as a fluorescent sensor for CTC, offering both high selectivity and sensitivity. The fluorescence quenching intensity showed a strong linear correlation (R 2 = 0.995) with CTC concentration in the range of 0–0.125 mmol/L. We further investigated the mechanism underlying CTC detection by G-CDs, revealing a combined effect of the static quenching and the inner filter effect (IFE). To explore practical applications, we incorporated the G-CDs into a polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) matrix to create a PVA/G-CDs fluorescent hybrid polymer film, which exhibited excellent flexibility and optical properties. This research not only expands the application scope of highly fluorescent carbon dots but also provides novel insights into CTC detection methods and the practical implementation of fluorescent films, potentially paving the way for advanced sensing technologies and materials.

Ethylenediamine, methyl orange, L-ascorbic acid, glutamic acid and anhydrous ethanol were purchased from Chengdu Cologne Chemical Co., Ltd. Amoxicillin was purchased from Shanghai McLean Biochemical Co., Ltd. Phenylalanine, histidine, lysine and chlortetracycline were supplied by Shanghai Aladdin Chemical Co., Ltd. L-threonine and L-hydroxyproline were ordered from Shanghai Yuanju Biotechnology Co., Ltd. All reagents used were of analytical grade to ensure experimental precision. In addition, the experimental water was double distilled water.

The Fourier transform infrared spectrum (FT-IR) of CDs was obtained by BRUKER VERTEX 70 FT-IR spectrometer. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra were recorded using a Shunyu Hengping 756 PC spectrophotometer. Fluorescence emission spectra were acquired with a Prism F98 fluorescence spectrophotometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed on a Thermo ESCALAB 250 XI multi-functional imaging electron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific).

The G-CDs were prepared by a simple hydrothermal method: weighing 0.1 g of methyl orange into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave, followed by the addition of 500 μL of ethylenediamine and 10 mL of deionized water. After thorough mixing, the solution was sonicated for 10 min to ensure homogeneity. The autoclave was then sealed and heated in an oven at 200 °C for 8 h. Subsequently, the obtained product solution was subjected to centrifugation at 15000 rpm for 15 min to remove any larger particles or aggregates. The final purification step involved slowly introducing the supernatant into a pre-packed silica gel column for chromatographic separation, ultimately yielding the purified G-CDs solution. The synthetic scheme and the proposed reaction mechanism for the formation of G-CDs were illustrated in Fig. 1 a and b respectively, providing insight into the structural evolution and chemical transformations occurring during the synthesis process.

The preparation of the PVA/G-CDs composite film is illustrated in Fig. 2 . Initially, 2 g of polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) was dispersed in 18 mL of distilled water and continuously stirred. The mixture is then heated at 80 °C for 1 h to dissolve the PVA. Next, 2 mL of G-CDs solution was added to the 18 mL of PVA aqueous solution, and the mixture is stirred for 20 min. Finally, the mixed solution is transferred to a clean culture plate and dried in an oven at 45 °C for 12 h. After drying, the PVA/G-CDs composite film was obtained by peeling it off from the culture plate.

To optimize the preparation conditions for G-CDs, we examined the effects of reaction time and temperature on their fluorescence performance. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the fluorescence intensity of G-CDs increased with temperature, peaking at 200 °C within the range of 100–200 °C. Fig. 3b illustrates the fluorescence spectra of G-CDs synthesized over various reaction times from 1 to 8 h, revealing that the fluorescence intensity reached its maximum at 8 h. In summary, the luminescence performance of the G-CDs was optimized with a reaction temperature of 200 °C and a reaction time of 8 h, yielding G-CDs with optimal fluorescence performance.

The structure and chemical composition of G-CDs were analyzed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The morphology of G-CDs was observed by TEM. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the particles of G-CDs are irregularly granular with an average particle size of about 7.8 nm. HRTEM (inset) shows that the lattice spacing of G-CDs is 0.21 nm, which is consistent with the graphene crystal plane (100). The chemical composition and functional groups of G-CDs were studied by FT-IR and XPS analysis. In the FT-IR spectrum of Fig. 4b, the peak at 3442 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of NH or OH, the peak at 1629 cm−1 indicates the existence of CC or CO, the peak at 1010 cm−1 is attributed to the bending vibration of C-N (indicating the successful incorporation of nitrogen atoms into C points), and the absorption at 613 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of C-S. The survey spectrum of XPS is shown in Fig. 4c. There are three strong peaks at 532 and 400.2 eV, and a weak peak at 286 eV, corresponding to O1s, C1s and N1s, respectively, which confirms the existence of O-rich functional groups and N-containing substances in G-CDs. The relative element contents were 64.45 %, 15.01 %, 19.15 % and 1.38 %, respectively. The high-resolution spectrum of the C1s signal is shown in Fig. 4d. There are three peaks at 284.6, 285.7 and 288.8 eV, representing CC/CC, CN and CO groups, respectively. Fig. 4e is the high-resolution spectrum of N1s, and two relative nitrogen states of pyridine N (398.5 eV) and graphite N (400.2 eV) can be clearly seen, which confirms that N is successfully doped in G-CDs. Fig. 4f shows the high-resolution spectrum of O1s, and the O1s peak at 532.6 eV is caused by the CO bond. These results also clearly confirmed the presence of hydroxyl, carbonyl and carboxyl groups on the surface of G-CDs.

The three-dimensional excitation and emission spectrum and its two-dimensional projection of the of G-CDs are shown in Fig. 5 a and b, respectively. It is evident that the excitation range of G-CDs primarily spans from 320 to 480 nm, while the emission range is concentrated between 430 and 550 nm. Fig. 5c presents the UV–Vis absorption spectra along with the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra, revealing that the maximum excitation peak of G-CDs occurs at 420 nm, with the corresponding maximum emission wavelength at 500 nm. The UV–Vis spectrum displays a broad optical absorption peak in the ultraviolet region, which may be attributed to the π-π* and n-π* electron transitions of the conjugated carbonyl groups (C–C/C–O) present in G-CDs. The optimal excitation wavelength for G-CDs is 365 nm, and the optimal emission wavelength is 437 nm. The insets in Fig. 5c show photographs of the G-CDs solution under sunlight (left) and 395 nm ultraviolet light (right), where the solution appears transparent, clear in sunlight, but exhibits strong green fluorescence under UV light, visually demonstrating its excellent green fluorescence performance. Fig. 5d shows the normalized emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths, and as the excitation wavelength increases from 395 nm to 445 nm, the emission peak remains nearly unchanged. This indicates that the G-CDs exhibit excitation-independent emissions, suggesting that the fluorescence likely arises from a homogeneous electronic structure or consistent energy gap transitions.

To assess the luminescent properties of G-CDs, the quantum yields of G-CDs were measured in different solvent systems. Solvents including acetonitrile (ACN), isopropanol (IPA), ethanol (EtOH), acetone (ACE), tetrahydrofuran (THF), water (WAT), and N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF) were used to plot absorbance curves based on integrated fluorescence intensity, as illustrated in Fig. 6 a. The slope of the fitted lines in the graph directly correlates with quantum yield, with steeper slopes indicating higher quantum yields. Using quinine sulfate (REF) as a reference, the quantum yields of G-CDs in different solvents were calculated, as shown in Fig. 6b. Notably, G-CDs in DMF exhibited a quantum yield as high as 37.1 %. Table S1 lists the quantum yields of various green carbon dots reported in the literature, clearly showing that the G-CDs synthesized in this study possess a significantly higher quantum yield compared to most previously reported green carbon dots.

Generally, the fluorescence stability of carbon dots is crucial for their broader applications. To evaluate the fluorescence stability of the synthesized G-CDs, the effects of reaction system parameters such as salt concentration, exposure duration, temperature, and pH on fluorescence intensity were investigated. As shown in Fig. 7 a, the fluorescence intensity of G-CDs exhibited minimal variation within a salt concentration range of 0–500 mM, demonstrating excellent salt tolerance. Fig. 7b illustrates the change in fluorescence intensity under continuous UV light exposure, showing only a slight decrease in intensity. Fig. 7c depicts the variation of G-CDs' fluorescence intensity with increasing temperature, where it maintained significant stability with almost no change. Fig. 7d presents the variation in fluorescence intensity over one week, indicating no significant reduction, further confirming the temperature robustness of G-CDs. Fig. S1 displays the influence of pH on G-CDs' fluorescence intensity; the graph indicates that at pH levels between 5 and 11, the fluorescence intensity remains strong and relatively stable. However, in strongly acidic or alkaline conditions, the fluorescence intensity decreases, suggesting that G-CDs exhibit good fluorescence stability within a certain pH range. Overall, G-CDs demonstrate outstanding performance, including high salt tolerance, strong light stability, significant resistance to temperature fluctuations, and long-term storage stability.

Given the exceptional photoluminescent properties, abundant surface functional groups, and excellent chemical stability of G-CDs, they present themselves as promising candidates for sensing materials. To investigate their potential, we conducted a comprehensive study on the fluorescence quenching effects of various substances on G-CDs in aqueous solution under 420 nm excitation. As illustrated in Fig. 8 a, chlortetracycline (CTC) exhibited a significant fluorescence quenching effect on G-CDs, while other antibiotics, amino acids, and metal ions had negligible impact on the fluorescence intensity. Furthermore, we examined the fluorescence intensity changes of G-CDs in the presence of CTC coexisting with 22 potential interfering substances. Fig. 8b clearly demonstrates that these interfering substances did not significantly affect the quenching action of CTC on G-CDs, indicating excellent anti-interference capability for CTC detection. As shown in Fig. 8c, the fluorescence intensity of G-CDs gradually decreased with increasing CTC concentration in the range of 0–0.2 mmol/L. Fig. 8d reveals a good linear relationship between (F 0-F)/F 0 and CTC concentration in the range of 0–0.125 mmol/L, where F and F 0 represent the fluorescence intensities of G-CDs with and without CTC, respectively. The linear equation was determined to be (F 0-F)/F 0=5.0101C CTC(mmol/L)+0.021, with a correlation coefficient R 2 of 0.995. Based on the calculation of 3δ/k (where δ is the standard deviation of six blank samples and k is the slope of the linear equation), the minimum detection limit of this method was found to be 0.12 μmol/L, demonstrating the high sensitivity of G-CDs for CTC detection.

As shown in Table 1 , the detection method used in this experiment was compared with other methods reported in the literature, such as enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA), high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), capillary electrophoresis (CE), and surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS). Although these methods exhibit low detection limits, they are limited in certain applications due to drawbacks such as expensive equipment, complex sample preparation, susceptibility to interference, and high maintenance costs. In contrast, the fluorescence spectroscopy method employed in this study offers significant advantages, including high sensitivity, rapid detection, low cost, and real-time monitoring capabilities. Furthermore, the effectiveness of G-CDs in detecting CTC was compared with that of other reported carbon dots. The G-CDs synthesized in this study demonstrated a broader detection range and lower detection limit for CTC, highlighting their superior performance. In summary, the G-CDs prepared in this study not only exhibit high selectivity and strong anti-interference ability for CTC detection but also achieve a low detection limit, making them a promising fluorescent probe for detecting CTC in real samples.

To validate the practical applicability of our method, we analyzed CTC levels in real water samples collected from both a lake and a pond, and conducted spiked recovery experiments to evaluate the method’s accuracy and reliability. As indicated in Table 2 , chlortetracycline was not detected in the original lake or pond water samples. In the spiked samples, recovery rates ranged from 97.6 % to 102.3 % in lake water and 94.0 % to 97.3 % in pond water, with relative standard deviations (RSD) between 1.8 % and 3.6 % for lake water and 2.5 % to 4.4 % for pond water. These findings confirm that the G-CD-based method delivers sufficient accuracy and precision for the analysis of real environmental samples, meeting the stringent requirements for practical analytical applications.

To investigate the reason behind the fluorescence quenching of G-CDs by chlortetracycline (CTC), we first measured the UV absorption spectra of G-CDs, CTC, and G-CDs combined with CTC. As shown in Fig. 9 a, the absorption peaks of CTC (in red) are primarily located around 240 nm, 260 nm, and 375 nm. Upon the addition of CTC to the G-CDs solution, the absorption spectrum (in blue) displayed significant changes. This phenomenon may result from the formation of a non-fluorescent ground-state complex between G-CDs and CTC, a type of fluorescence quenching known as static quenching [37,38]. To further validate this hypothesis, we also examined the changes in fluorescence lifetime of G-CDs before and after the addition of CTC. As shown in Fig. 9b, the difference in fluorescence lifetime with and without CTC was only 0.1 ns, indicating minimal change. This further confirms that the quenching process is primarily static rather than dynamic quenching or fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). Additionally, as illustrated in Fig. 9c, the comparison of the absorption spectrum of CTC with the maximum excitation spectrum of G-CDs reveals overlap, suggesting that CTC may also induce fluorescence quenching of G-CDs through the IFE [38]. In summary, the fluorescence quenching of G-CDs by CTC results from the combined effects of static quenching and the IFE.

The solid-state fluorescence of CDs is particularly important in the field of optoelectronics. The fluorescence properties of solid-state CDs are difficult to achieve the effect in solution, and even fluorescence quenching occurs. In order to solve the problem of fluorescence quenching, an acceptable and effective method is to disperse CDs in the polymer matrix. In this work, PVA was selected as the dispersion matrix of G-CDs. As shown in Fig. 10 a and b, the PVA/G-CDs film exhibits bright green fluorescence under 395 nm UV light (Fig. 10a) and maintains high transparency under visible light (Fig. 10b), indicating excellent dispersion of G-CDs within the PVA matrix. These strong optical properties suggest great potential for practical applications. Additionally, the flexibility of the PVA/G-CDs film was tested by folding and curling, as shown in Fig. 10c–e, with results demonstrating good flexibility, further enhancing its applicability in real-world environments. Fig. 11 a presents the UV–Vis absorption spectra of the PVA/G-CDs and PVA films, where the PVA/G-CDs film shows much higher UV absorption, extending even into the visible light range, with particularly strong enhancement in the UV region. As shown in Fig. 11b, the PVA/G-CDs film also display excitation-independent emissions, similar to the G-CDs in aqueous solution (Fig. 5d). These observations indicate that G-CDs serve as an excellent additive in polymer materials, providing enhanced UV resistance and optical performance.

In summary, high-performance green fluorescent carbon dots (G-CDs) were successfully synthesized using methyl orange as a carbon source and ethylenediamine as a nitrogen dopant through a simple hydrothermal method. The resulting G-CDs exhibited an impressive quantum yield of 37.1 %, with optimal excitation and emission wavelengths of 420 nm and 500 nm, respectively. These G-CDs demonstrated excellent fluorescence properties, including strong salt tolerance and photostability. Due to the selective fluorescence quenching of G-CDs by chlortetracycline (CTC) and the high sensitivity of this response, G-CDs were utilized as a chemical sensor for CTC detection. A linear relationship was observed between the fluorescence intensity of G-CDs and the concentration of CTC, with the equation (F 0-F)/F 0=5.0101C CTC(mmol/L)+0.021, R 2=0.995, and a detection limit of 0.12 μmol/L. This method was successfully applied for CTC determination in lake water, achieving recovery rates of 97.6 %–102.3 % and 94 %–97.3 %, with relative standard deviations of 1.8 %–3.6 % and 2.5 %–4.4 %, respectively. Additionally, a PVA/G-CD composite film was prepared by dispersing the G-CDs in a polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) matrix. This PVA/G-CD film retained the unique optical properties of the G-CDs, exhibiting strong green fluorescence and high visible light transparency, thereby expanding the potential applications of fluorescent carbon dots.
Not applicable.
Jing Hu: Investigation, Writing – original draft. Xiang Long: Investigation. Shaogui Wu: Conceptualization, Supervision, Investigation, Writing – review & editing.
Not applicable.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Thank Prof. Dongmao Zhang, Ph.D Joanna Xiuzhu Xu, Xue Leng et al. in Department of Chemistry, Mississippi State University for helpful advices.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2024.125564.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
